## Requirement Concepts

<rq-trace parent_id="93f47c" parent_rel="trace">
**Business Requirement**: A Business Requirement is a requirement that specifies characteristics of the business process that must be satisfied by the system.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="93f47c" parent_rel="trace">
**Design Constraint**: A Design Constraint is a requirement that specifies a constraint on the implementation of a system or on a part of it.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="dcb5ec" parent_rel="trace">
**Extended Requirement**: An Extended Requirement is a standard Requirement subtype, which adds some properties to a requirement element. These properties such as a source, risk, and verify method are important for the requirement management. Specific projects should add their own properties. All these properties are now available in the standard Requirement Specification window and Requirements Table. If any of these property values is specified, a requirement is automatically converted to ExtendedRequirement.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="93f47c" parent_rel="trace">
**Functional Requirement**: A Functional Requirement is a requirement that specifies a behavior that a system or a part of a system must perform.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="93f47c" parent_rel="trace">
**Interface Requirement**: An Interface Requirement is a requirement that specifies the ports for connecting systems and parts of a system. Optionally, it may include the items that flow across the connector and/or the Interface constraints.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="93f47c" parent_rel="trace">
**Performance Requirement**: A Performance Requirement refers to a requirement that quantitatively measures the extent to which a system or a system part satisfy a required capability or condition.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="93f47c" parent_rel="trace">
**Physical Requirement**: A Physical Requirement specifies the physical characteristics and/or physical constraints of a system or a system part.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="2ad50f" parent_rel="trace">
**ReqIF**: The Requirement Interchange Format (ReqIF) standard is an XML based international standard adopted by Object Management Group (OMG) as a formal specification for exchanging requirements between software tools from different vendors.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="e8c152" parent_rel="trace">
**Requirement**: A Requirement specifies a capability or a condition that must (or should) be satisfied. Requirements are used to establish a contract between the customer (or other stakeholder) and those responsible for designing and implementing the system. A requirement may specify a function that a system must perform or a performance condition a system must achieve. A requirement can also appear on other diagrams to show its relationship to other modeling elements. A standard requirement includes properties to specify its unique identifier and text requirement. When a requirement nests other requirements, all the nested requirements apply as a part of the container requirement (the requirement that contains all the nested requirements).
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="5b24dd" parent_rel="trace">
**Requirement Diagram**: Requirements Diagrams provide modeling constructs to represent text-based requirements and relate them to other modeling elements. These requirement modeling constructs are intended to provide a bridge between traditional requirement management tools and other UML models. Requirements Diagrams display requirements, packages, other classifiers, test cases, rationales, and relationships.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="5b24dd" parent_rel="trace">
**Requirement Specification**: A represented collection of requirements that are related by a user decision or needs. A Requirement Specification may represent requirements for a system, a component, or other subjects. In a means of MagicDraw, a Requirement Specification is a package of requirements.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="5b24dd" parent_rel="trace">
**Requirement Table**: A Requirements Table represents textual requirements in a table format: each row represent a requirement and you can specify what columns could be in a table according your needs. A new table consists of three columns by default: a requirement ID, name, and textual description.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="87d265" parent_rel="verify">
**Test Case**: A Test Case (Activity / StateMachine / Interaction) is a method for verifying a requirement.
</rq-trace>

<rq-trace parent_id="93f47c" parent_rel="trace">
**Usability Requirement**: A Usability Requirement specifies the fitness for use of a system for its users and other actors.
</rq-trace>
